---
title: "Homework 9"
format: html
---
```{r}
### Homework 9 ###
# Sofia Puccio #
# 03/18/2026 #

# In this assigment I will be creating figures in ggplot corresponding to the four major statistical designs reflecting relationships between y and x. 

# Question 1: Continuous (x) and Continuous (y) 
# In this segment, both variables are continuous
# A relationship is shown with a regression line 

library(ggplot2)

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Sepal.Length, y = Petal.Length)) +
  geom_point(color = "steelblue", size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red", se = TRUE) +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Sepal Length and Petal Length in Iris",
    x = "Sepal Length (cm)",
    y = "Petal Length (cm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Question 2: Discrete (x) and Continuous (y) 
# Comparing sepal lengths across iris species 
# The species is categorical, and the sepal length is the continuous variable 

ggplot(iris, aes(x = Species, y = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(
    title = "Sepal Length Distribution Across Iris Species",
    x = "Species",
    y = "Sepal Length (cm)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# The result is a series of boxplots that show distribution of sepal length by species with individual points.
# ANOVA style visualization comparing the distribution of sepal length across species

# Question 3: Continuous (x) and Discrete (y)
# This can be shown in a logistic regression curve 
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = mpg, y = am)) +
  geom_jitter(height = 0.05, width = 0, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "glm",
              method.args = list(family = "binomial"),
              color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Probability of Manual Transmission vs MPG",
    x = "Miles per Gallon (mpg)",
    y = "Transmission (0 = Automatic, 1 = Manual)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# This results in a logistic growth curve that includes binary points with logistic regression curve displaying probability of manual transmission increasing with MPG.

# Question 4: Discrete (x) Discrete (y)
# For this we will use the number of cylinders v. transmission type, with cylinders and transmission types both being categorical

mtcars$cyl <- as.factor(mtcars$cyl)
mtcars$am <- as.factor(mtcars$am)

ggplot(mtcars, aes(x = cyl, fill = am)) +
  geom_bar(position = "dodge") +
  labs(
    title = "Transmission Type by Number of Cylinders",
    x = "Number of Cylinders",
    y = "Count",
    fill = "Transmission (0 = Auto, 1 = Manual)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# The result here is grouped bar charts showing counts of manual vs automatic cars for each cylinder category.

```
